subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content:
#### Player-Count Reference
- **Standard game** — 2 to 6 players.
- **Recommended** — 4 players.

#### Core Components
- **Category list cards** — Multiple list cards, each with 12 categories (e.g., "A boy's name," "Something cold," "A vacation destination").
- **Letter die** — 1 twenty-sided die with letters of the alphabet (some letters excluded).
- **Sand timer** — 1 three-minute timer.
- **Answer pads** — Pads for each player to write answers.
- **Pencils** — 1 per player.
- **Folder/clip** — Card holder to display the current category list.
- **Rulebook** — 1 rulebook.

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content:
#### Setup Sequence
1. **Distribute answer pads** — Give each player an answer pad and pencil.
2. **Select category list** — Choose a category list card and place it in the holder so all players can see the 12 categories.
3. **Place timer** — Set the 3-minute timer in the center.
4. **Prepare letter die** — Place the 20-sided letter die within reach.

#### Table Layout
- **Category list** — Visible to all players at all times.
- **Writing space** — Each player needs room to write privately.
- **Timer** — Central, visible to everyone.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
#### Individual Play
- **No teams** — Each player competes individually.
- **Equipment** — Each player has an answer pad and pencil.
- **Privacy** — Players should not be able to see each other's answers during the timed writing phase.

#### Team Variant
- **Optional teams** — Players may form teams and collaborate on a single answer sheet.
- **Same rules apply** — Teams follow the same scoring rules as individual players.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content:
#### Pre-First-Turn Checks
- **Category list visible** — All 12 categories are displayed and readable.
- **Answer pads ready** — Each player has a numbered answer sheet (1-12).
- **Letter die ready** — Die is available for the first roll.

#### First Action
1. **Roll the letter die** — One player rolls the die. The letter shown is the required starting letter for all answers this round.
2. **Announce the letter** — Call out the letter clearly.
3. **Start the timer** — Flip the 3-minute timer.
4. **Begin writing** — All players simultaneously write answers for all 12 categories, each beginning with the rolled letter.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
#### Round Flow
1. **Roll letter** — Roll the 20-sided letter die to determine this round's letter.
2. **Start timer** — Begin the 3-minute countdown.
3. **Write answers** — All players simultaneously fill in answers for all 12 categories using words starting with the rolled letter.
4. **Timer expires** — All players stop writing immediately.
5. **Read and compare** — Go through each category one at a time. Each player reads their answer aloud.
6. **Challenge** — Any player may challenge an answer's validity. Group votes to accept or reject.
7. **Score** — Award points based on unique answers.
8. **Next round** — Select a new category list, roll a new letter, and repeat.

#### Game Length
- **Standard game** — 3 rounds with different category lists and letters.
- **Short game** — 1 or 2 rounds by agreement.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
#### Writing Rules
- **Starting letter** — Every answer must begin with the rolled letter.
- **One answer per category** — Write exactly one answer per category line.
- **Blank allowed** — You may leave a category blank if you cannot think of an answer.
- **Proper nouns allowed** — Names of people, places, brands, etc. are valid.
- **Creative answers encouraged** — Answers should be defensible but creative interpretations are part of the fun.

#### Scoring Rules
- **Unique answers** — Score 1 point for each answer that no other player also wrote.
- **Duplicate answers** — If two or more players wrote the same answer for the same category, none of those players score for that category.
- **Blank entries** — Score 0 points.
- **Alliterative bonus** — Answers where multiple words start with the rolled letter score bonus points (e.g., rolling "B" and writing "Billy Bob" for a name = 2 points instead of 1).

#### Challenges
- **Any player may challenge** — If an answer seems invalid or does not fit the category.
- **Group vote** — Majority decides if the answer counts.
- **Rejected answer** — Scores 0 points.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
#### Alliterative Bonus
- **Multi-word answers** — If an answer has multiple words each starting with the rolled letter, score 1 point per matching word instead of just 1 point total.
- **Example** — Letter is "S": writing "San Salvador" for a city scores 2 points (if unique).
- **First word must count** — The answer's first word must start with the rolled letter as a baseline.

#### Duplicate Elimination
- **Case insensitive** — "Paris" and "paris" count as the same answer.
- **Same meaning** — "NYC" and "New York City" are typically treated as duplicates by group consensus.
- **Different categories** — Duplicates only matter within the same category across players.

#### The Die
- **Letter distribution** — The 20-sided die excludes difficult letters (Q, U, V, X, Y, Z in most editions).
- **Re-roll** — If the same letter comes up as a previous round, re-roll.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
#### Game End
- **After 3 rounds** — Standard game ends after 3 complete rounds.
- **Variable** — Groups may agree to play more or fewer rounds.

#### Final Scoring
- **Sum all rounds** — Add each player's points from all rounds together.
- **Winner** — Player with the highest total score wins.

#### Tiebreaker
- **No official tiebreaker** — Play an additional sudden-death round with a single category, or share the victory.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content:
#### Speed vs. Creativity
- **Fill easy categories first** — Knock out obvious answers quickly to ensure you have something for most categories.
- **Return for creative answers** — After a quick pass, go back and try to replace common answers with unique ones.
- **Alliterative hunting** — Actively look for multi-word answers starting with the rolled letter for bonus points.

#### Time Management
- **Don't stall on blanks** — If nothing comes to mind for a category within 10 seconds, skip it and come back.
- **Prioritize quantity** — Having 10 basic answers beats having 5 creative ones if the creative answers turn out to be duplicates anyway.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
#### Frequent Errors
- **Writing the obvious answer** — The first answer everyone thinks of is the one most likely to be duplicated.
- **Overthinking** — Spending too long on one clever answer while leaving several categories blank.
- **Forgetting the letter** — In the rush, accidentally writing answers that start with the wrong letter.
- **Not challenging** — Letting questionable answers slide gives opponents free points.
- **Ignoring alliterative bonus** — Missing easy opportunities for double points on multi-word answers.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
#### Decision 1: Safe vs. Unique Answers
- **Safe answer** — High chance of being correct/valid but likely duplicated (scores 0).
- **Unique answer** — Less obvious, higher chance of being the only one (scores 1+), but may be challenged.

#### Decision 2: Speed vs. Quality
- **Speed** — Fill every category with something, even if common. Guarantees no blanks.
- **Quality** — Take time to think of unusual answers. Risk leaving some categories blank.

#### Decision 3: Challenge or Accept
- **Challenge** — If you suspect an answer is invalid, challenging it costs nothing and could remove a competitor's point.
- **Accept** — If the answer is borderline but the player can defend it, don't waste group goodwill on minor disputes.
